one_line_takeaway: An unsupervised, label-free defense for LLM multi-agent systems that spots malicious agents by contrasting them against synthetically corrupted normal behavior across ego/neighbor/global graph levels, then prunes their edges - matching or beating supervised defenses especially on unseen attack types.
key_finding: BlindGuard, trained with NO attack labels, approaches the supervised upper bound (G-Safeguard) and clearly beats generic unsupervised GAD baselines (DOMINANT, PREM, TAM), reaching AUC > 80% in most settings. Its headline result is generalization to UNSEEN attacks: when a model trained on prompt injection is tested against unseen memory attacks on CSQA (Table 2), BlindGuard beats the supervised G-Safeguard across topologies (e.g., Chain: BlindGuard AUC 73.33 / ASR@3 9.00 vs G-Safeguard AUC 59.56 / ASR@3 19.00), because the supervised method overfits to the known attack type while BlindGuard models normal behavior. Ablation confirms both neighbor-level and global-level features are needed; removing both causes severe degradation on PoisonRAG.
core_contribution: BlindGuard is an UNSUPERVISED defense for LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) that detects and isolates malicious agents without any attack-specific labels or prior knowledge of attack patterns. The paper's own contribution (distinct from the cited supervised baseline G-Safeguard, which it treats as prior work / an upper bound) has three parts: (1) it frames MAS safeguarding as an unsupervised graph anomaly detection (GAD) problem trained only on normal/benign interaction data; (2) a 'hierarchical agent encoder' that fuses agent-level (ego), neighbor-level, and global-system-level features via a summarization-transformation architecture to build agent representations; and (3) a 'corruption-guided attack detector' that synthesizes pseudo-malicious agents by injecting magnitude-scaled directional noise into normal SentenceBERT embeddings, then trains a detector with a supervised contrastive loss and scores agents at inference by negative average cosine similarity to all other agents. High-scoring agents are removed via bidirectional edge pruning.
threat_model: Adversary compromises a subset of agents (exactly 3 in experiments) within a collaborative MAS modeled as a directed graph; compromised agents behave normally on the surface but emit misleading messages that propagate through inter-agent communication ('propagation vulnerability') to corrupt collective decisions. Three attack modalities are considered, all following G-Safeguard's setup: (1) prompt injection (corrupting system or user prompts), (2) memory poisoning (injecting fabricated histories / poisoning knowledge bases), and (3) tool exploitation (abusing external plugins). Defender assumptions: access to a small amount of NORMAL (unattacked) MAS interaction data only; NO labeled malicious agents and NO knowledge of the attack type at training time. The defense is explicitly reactive/mitigative (runs after intrusion, before the next propagation round) rather than preventive.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluation is confined to SIMULATED MAS environments; open-world validation is left to future work. (2) As a detection-based approach, BlindGuard cannot PREVENT malicious agents from entering the system, only mitigate propagation after intrusion; it is positioned as a complementary layer, not a replacement for preventive defenses. Reviewer-observed limitations: results are not strictly monotone superiority over unsupervised baselines on every cell (e.g., on Chain TA/InjecAgent DOMINANT AUC 88.00 edges out BlindGuard 86.22), so 'competitive' is fair but not universal dominance; the corruption model (directional Gaussian noise in embedding space) is a synthetic proxy for real adversarial semantics, and the paper notes a residual gap between synthetic and real malicious agents; the fixed 'top-3 malicious' detection budget matches the fixed 3-compromised-agent setup, so behavior under unknown/variable attacker counts is not tested.

datasets_used: PoisonRAG (memory attack, also used for scalability and ablation)
quantitative_results: Setup: exactly 3 agents compromised; metrics AUC (detection, higher better) and ASR@3 (attack success after 3 rounds, lower better); GPT-4o-mini backbone for Table 1.
Star PI(CSQA), GPT-4o-mini: BlindGuard AUC 85.78 / ASR@3 22.00 vs No Defense ASR@3 50.33 vs G-Safeguard AUC 100.00 / ASR@3 19.33.
Chain PI(CSQA): BlindGuard AUC 80.00 / ASR@3 23.67 (No Defense ASR@3 42.33); unsupervised baselines DOMINANT 47.11 AUC, PREM 50.67 AUC, TAM 55.11 AUC.
Star PI(MMLU): BlindGuard AUC 86.22 / ASR@3 20.33 (No Defense ASR@3 39.00).
Star MA(PoisonRAG): BlindGuard AUC 89.33 / ASR@3 10.00 vs No Defense ASR@3 26.00.

Unseen-attack generalization (Table 2, trained on PI, tested on CSQA MA): Chain BlindGuard AUC 73.33 / ASR@3 9.00 vs G-Safeguard AUC 59.56 / ASR@3 19.00; Tree BlindGuard AUC 78.67 / ASR@3 8.00 vs G-Safeguard AUC 60.44 / ASR@3 16.70; Random BlindGuard AUC 78.22 / ASR@3 13.33 vs G-Safeguard AUC 60.44 / ASR@3 13.33.
Scalability on PoisonRAG (Table 3, ASR@3 by round): 20 agents R1/R2/R3 BlindGuard 3.51/4.54/5.57 vs No Defense 15.89/23.22/29.51; 50 agents BlindGuard 1.81/2.66/3.76 vs No Defense 5.67/16.31/20.92.
Case where a baseline edges BlindGuard: Chain TA(InjecAgent) DOMINANT AUC 88.00 vs BlindGuard AUC 86.22.
baselines_compared: G-Safeguard (supervised graph-based defense; used as upper bound since it trains on labeled attacked agents)
